serving.description: 1 set meal for 2 persons (fried pork with chili, stir-fried pea sprouts, 2 portions of rice, watermelon)
serving.natural_language_portion: One set meal for two persons comprising approximately 700 g total, including a shared plate of fried pork with chili, a side of stir-fried pea sprouts, two individual portions of steamed white rice, and a slice of watermelon.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Pork With Chili — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Fried Pork With Chili (cr_id G00010919). All per-100g values are sourced from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: The HPB source reports carbohydrate_g as 0.0 g/100g for the fried pork with chili component specifically. This reflects the pork-and-chili stir-fry portion only (no rice included in this per-100g figure). Because the carbohydrate content of the pork-chili component is negligible (0 g/100g), glycemic index is not meaningfully applicable to this component. A GI of 45 is assigned as an analogous estimate based on mixed pork stir-fry dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database, acknowledging that the full set meal includes white rice which carries a high GI (~72). Glycemic load per serving for the pork-chili component alone is 0.0 given 0 g carbohydrate. Consumers should note the full set meal GL is substantially higher due to the rice component.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; sodium at 1050 mg/100g is nearly double the 600 mg/100g upper limit, and even a modest 150 g serving delivers approximately 1575 mg sodium — exceeding two-thirds of the 2300 mg daily recommended maximum.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Pork-chili component has 0 g carbohydrate per 100g, but the full set meal includes white rice which significantly raises the overall glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: The fried pork with chili component itself presents negligible glycaemic load (0 g carbohydrate/100g), making it acceptable for blood glucose management; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit the accompanying white rice portion and request reduced stir-fry sauce to minimise hidden sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g), moderate fat (10.5 g/100g), and cholesterol (89 mg/100g) combined with frying preparation raise cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this dish due to its high sodium content (1050 mg/100g), moderate total fat (10.5 g/100g), and cholesterol (89 mg/100g); the frying method also generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that may exacerbate endothelial dysfunction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork (arachidonic acid source), frying-generated AGEs, high sodium, and chili (nightshade) collectively promote inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; pork is rich in arachidonic acid which amplifies inflammatory cytokine production, frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs, sodium at 1050 mg/100g worsens tissue swelling, and chili as a nightshade vegetable may trigger individual joint flares.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Pork with Chili Set (2 Pax) is a Singaporean Chinese hawker-style set meal designed for two persons, comprising a shared plate of stir-fried or pan-fried pork slices cooked with fresh chili peppers, a side dish of stir-fried pea sprouts (豆苗), two individual portions of steamed white rice, and a serving of watermelon as a light dessert. The pork is typically marinated in soy sauce, oyster sauce, and garlic before being wok-fried at high heat, producing a savoury, slightly spicy, and umami-rich flavour profile with a tender yet slightly caramelised texture. The dish is a staple of Singapore's hawker centre culture and is commonly ordered at lunch and dinner. Nutritionally, the pork-chili component is high in protein (19.1 g/100g) and moderate in fat (10.5 g/100g) with negligible carbohydrate (0 g/100g) in the pork component alone; however, the full set meal's glycaemic load is substantially elevated by the accompanying white rice. Sodium is very high at 1050 mg/100g, making this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The cholesterol content of 89 mg/100g and the frying preparation method are additional considerations for cardiovascular health. The dish is not suitable for halal or kosher diets due to the pork content.

Sugar_g is confirmed zero per the same HPB source. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source for this pork-chili component. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, vitamin profile, and moisture data for this specific hawker-style fried pork with chili preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (cr_id G00010919) as of 2026. GI assigned as analogous estimate (45, low) based on mixed pork stir-fry dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; the pork-chili component itself has 0 g carbohydrate/100g making GI not directly applicable to this component in isolation — the full set meal GL is substantially higher due to white rice. The set meal is not halal due to pork content. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03315671 (pork dish) and FoodEx2 A0CKP (Pork meat, cooked) used as broader-class anchors; no exact codes exist for this specific preparation.
